chief_complaint: it it burns when I pee

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 4-day history of burning sensation during urination localized to her genital area. She reports the symptom has remained consistent without improvement and is associated with increased urinary frequency. She has not tried any treatments so far. She has a known allergy to penicillin and a history of renal disease. She is not pregnant. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only evaluation.

A: 
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute uncomplicated cystitis 
- Differential: 
 1. Urinary tract infection (including cystitis) 
 2. Urethritis (possibly sexually transmitted infection) 
 3. Vaginitis or vulvovaginitis 
 4. Interstitial cystitis or other non-infectious causes of dysuria 
- Red flags considered: No fever, flank pain, or systemic symptoms reported to suggest pyelonephritis or complicated UTI.

P: 
- Disposition: Patient can be managed outpatient with close follow-up given stable symptoms and no systemic signs of infection. 
- Plan: 
 1. Recommend urine analysis and urine culture to confirm diagnosis and guide antibiotic therapy. 
 2. Given penicillin allergy and history of renal disease, consider prescribing nitrofurantoin or fosfomycin as first-line agents for uncomplicated cystitis, pending culture results. 
 3. Advise increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief measures such as urinary analgesics if needed. 
 4. Educate patient on signs of worsening infection including fever, flank pain, or inability to tolerate oral intake, which would require urgent evaluation. 
- Safety net: Instruct patient to seek immediate care if she develops fever, chills, flank pain, worsening dysuria, or any new systemic symptoms. Follow-up in 48-72 hours if symptoms persist or worsen.

evidence.title: Burn Evaluation: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
ep into the skin 
Is larger than 3 inches across 
Looks leathery and dry 
Has patches of white, brown, or black 
Swells very fast 
Gets more painful over time, has pus, or causes a fever , which may be signs of an infection 
Also, get medical help for all burns that:
Are caused by electricity or chemicals 
Are on the face, hands, genitals, buttocks, over a joint, or go all the way around a part of the body, such as a leg or the chest 
Happen with inhalation (breathing in) of smoke, hot gasses, or chemicals 
Happen to a child under age 5 or an adult older than age 70 
References 
Agrawal A, Raibagkar SC, Vora HJ. Friction Burns: Epidemiology and Prevention. Ann Burns Fire Disasters [Internet]. 2008 Mar 31 [cited 2023 Mar 8]; 21(1): 3-6. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3188131 
American Burn Association [Internet]. Chicago (IL): American Burn Association; c2022

Conditions A to Z
Cystitis 
Cystitis is a urinary tract infection (UTI) that affects the bladder. It's common, particularly in women. It often gets better by itself, but may sometimes be treated with antibiotics. 
Some people get cystitis frequently and may need regular or long-term treatment.
Symptoms of cystitis 
Symptoms of cystitis include:
pain, burning or stinging when you pee needing to pee more often and urgently than usual pee that's dark, cloudy or strong smelling pain low down in your tummy Symptoms in young children may also include:
a high temperature – they feel hotter than usual if you touch their neck, back or tummy wetting themselves reduced appetite and being s

evidence.title: Epididymitis - NHS
evidence.snippet:
use, you may have other symptoms, such as difficulty peeing, or a white, yellow or green discharge from the tip of the penis.
Causes of epididymitis 
Epididymitis is usually caused by a sexually transmitted infection (STI) , such as chlamydia or gonorrhoea . This is more likely in younger men under 35 years old. In men over 35, the main cause is usually a urine infection.
If testing shows that your epididymitis is caused by a STI, it may be recommended that your current or recent sexual partners (over the past 6 months) are also tested for STIs.
It can also be caused by a urinary tract infection (UTI) , but UTIs are less common in men.
A UTI
